question_text:
Hi, I just transferred to the Zurich branch this morning and my setup is completely broken.
First, when I dial external vendor numbers on the desktop app, the line is dead silent—I can't hear them at all, though they say they hear me.
Second, I'm locked out of the 'ERP_Upgrade' directory on the intranet; it just says I don't have permission, which makes no sense since I viewed it yesterday.
Finally, I tried to send a scanned contract from the hallway printer to a vendor's email. The printer screen showed a green checkmark, but the vendor never received the email. Please fix my account!
answer: Summary - what the records explicitly document - Scan-to-mail failing for external recipients can be caused by the printer being in a VLAN that is not allowed by the SMTP relay. In the documented case, the printer showed no error on its display (green check), the SMTP logs showed "Relay denied", and the fix was to add the printer VLAN IP range to the relay allow list and verify DNS resolution. After that external delivery worked and the ticket was closed [Data: Sources (6)]. - Users losing access while others keep access has been caused by explicit Deny ACEs applied to a resource (in the documented case a printer queue). Removing the explicit Deny and logging the user back in restored access [Data: Sources (3)]. - Changes during a relocation or reorganization (new AD groups / group membership change) have produced permission and printer-default issues; correcting AD group membership according to the standard resolved those cases [Data: Sources (2, 3)]. - Network segmentation changes (new VLANs or VM restores / Windows updates) have previously caused printing and share-access disruptions; fixes included adjusting routing/firewall rules and re-enabling network shares on print servers after a VM restore [Data: Sources (22, 28)]. Step‑by‑step resolution plan (tasks that follow documented fixes) 1) Resolve scan-to-mail (hallway printer -> vendor email) - Check the SMTP relay logs for the printer's IP to see if the relay denied the message (look for "Relay denied"). The indexed record shows this exact symptom and log message in the prior case [Documented action]. This is supported by the case where the printer showed a green check but external delivery failed and the SMTP log showed "Relay denied" [Data: Sources (6)]. - Verify the printer's IP and VLAN. In the prior case the printer was placed into a newly defined printer VLAN which was not in the relay allow list [Data: Sources (6)]. - If the printer VLAN is not in the SMTP relay allow list, add the VLAN's IP range to the relay allow list and ensure DNS resolution is internal as required by the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard [Documented action]. After this change the prior case reported successful external delivery and ticket closure [Data: Sources (6)]. - Test: scan to an internal mailbox (should succeed) and scan to an external mailbox (Gmail/partner) - confirm external delivery [Documented action & test steps used previously] [Data: Sources (6)]. 2) Restore access to intranet directory 'ERP_Upgrade' - Confirm the user's AD group memberships and compare to the expected group(s) for Zurich branch access. The indexed records show that moves into different AD groups during reorganizations have caused access/default mapping issues and needed correction [Documented action]. This is supported by the documented group-change incident where group membership caused incorrect printer defaults until cleaned up [Data: Sources (2)]. - Check the folder ACL for explicit Deny entries for the user account. In a documented incident an explicit "Deny" ACE on a print queue for a user caused an immediate "Access denied" and removing that deny restored access [Documented action] [Data: Sources (3)]. - If an explicit Deny is present, remove it (and ensure permissions are assigned via AD groups as required by the standard). After change, have the user log off and log on again and then retry access [Documented action; analogous workflow in prior case] [Data: Sources (3)]. - If group membership is incorrect (e.g., user was added to a Zurich or restrictive group during the transfer), correct the group membership according to the organisation's blueprint/standard, and then verify access [Documented action; group-cleanup resolution in prior case] [Data: Sources (2)]. - Test: user attempts to open ERP_Upgrade after relogin; confirm access. 3) Voice call audio (desktop app): silent incoming audio - The indexed records do not contain any documented cases or troubleshooting steps for VoIP/desktop-app one‑way audio or silent call legs. I do not have evidence in the indexed records to show how such issues were diagnosed or fixed in prior incidents. Therefore I cannot present a step‑by‑step remediation drawn from prior cases for this specific symptom. (No supporting sources available.) - Recommended (not documented in the indexed records): run basic VoIP diagnostics - confirm the user's location/AD group change didn't alter network/firewall/NAT rules, verify headset/audio device settings and client audio outputs, capture a client-side call log and check for packet loss/codec negotiation problems, and escalate to the telephony team if network/codec issues are suspected. Marking these as recommended because they are NOT taken from the indexed records. Additional checks that are documented as relevant to relocation/change events - Because the user just transferred branches, configuration changes (AD group change, VLAN assignment, firewall/routing differences) are plausible causes for both the intranet permission issue and the scan-to-mail failure. Prior tickets show that relocation/group changes and VLAN/firewall rules were root causes in similar cases [Data: Sources (2, 6, 22)]. - If server-side shares or print servers were recently restored/updated (VM restore or Windows update), network share re-enable steps were required for printers in at least one documented incident - if you see VM or patch events in the Zurich infrastructure timeline, consider verifying recent updates/restores on relevant servers and re-enabling shares if needed [Documented action] [Data: Sources (28)]. What I can do now (based on documented procedures) and what I cannot - I can apply the documented, proven steps for the scan-to-mail problem: check SMTP logs, confirm printer VLAN, add VLAN to relay allow list, and re-test - this sequence fixed the previous incident [Data: Sources (6)]. - I can apply the documented, proven steps for the access permission problem: inspect ACLs for explicit Deny ACEs and verify/correct AD group membership, then re-test after relogin - these actions fixed prior incidents [Data: Sources (3, 2)]. - I cannot provide a remediation sequence taken from the indexed records for the VoIP silent audio symptom because no prior cases addressing desktop-app call audio are present in the indexed knowledge. Any troubleshooting steps I propose for the VoIP issue are recommendations only, not documented fixes from the records. Please confirm and authorize these immediate actions - Permission to check the SMTP relay logs and, if required, add the printer VLAN IP range to the relay allow list (I will record the change and request re-test) - supported by the documented fix [Data: Sources (6)]. - Permission to inspect the ERP_Upgrade folder ACL and the user's AD group memberships and to remove any explicit Deny or correct the group membership per the standard - supported by documented fixes [Data: Sources (3, 2)]. - For the VoIP issue: do you want me to open an escalation to the telephony team and collect the client call logs (this is a recommended next step, not one documented in the indexed records)? If you want, I can proceed with the documented fixes for the printer/SMTP and the directory permissions now. If you prefer escalation for the VoIP audio, I will log a separate ticket and collect the necessary client logs and system info for the telephony team (recommended, not documented in the indexed knowledge).

question_text: URGENT MAJOR INCIDENT: We are getting flooded with calls from the Sales and Finance teams after the HR department updated the organizational chart in the system last night. First, our freelance consultants are complaining they are locked out of their shared workspaces and get an error screen. Second, the sales reps are trying to call clients, but the dialer just beeps and drops the call immediately. Finally, the finance team in Zurich is furious because their confidential invoices are physically printing out in the Basel office. What on earth did the org chart update break?
answer: Summary - what the indexed records explicitly document - A recent organisational/group change caused default printers to be reassigned because AD group membership was inconsistent after the reorganisation. In that prior incident the root cause was "inconsistent group membership following an organizational adjustment"; remediation was to correct group membership according to the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard, run a policy refresh and confirm the default stayed correct [Data: Sources (2)]. - Other prior printing incidents in the records show two additional, different causes and fixes you may want to keep in mind: - A print server network share broke after a Windows update + VM restore; the engineers fixed the network share (disabled/re-enabled the share for all queues) and pending documents printed successfully [Data: Sources (28, 29)]. - A newly introduced printer VLAN blocked the print server because a firewall rule did not allow the print-server network to reach the printer VLAN; adjusting the firewall/routing per the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard restored printing [Data: Sources (22)]. - Authentication/connectivity problems caused by misconfigured ADFS client parameters (EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) were resolved by aligning ADFS client parameters with the TEST environment; that change restored connectivity for the affected application [Data: Sources (11)]. What the records do NOT document - There is no record in the indexed knowledge about a dialer/telephony system failing (calls beeping and dropping) caused by an org-chart/AD-group update. The indexed tickets do not document a prior dialer outage or a proven fix for that exact symptom. Therefore I cannot provide a documented, guaranteed fix for the dialer from the records. Concrete, step‑by‑step resolution plan (distinguish documented actions vs. recommended actions) A. Immediate triage (documented checks and actions to run first) 1) Confirm which AD groups were changed by the HR/org‑chart update and which user accounts or groups were deactivated/created. - Rationale from records: printer misassignment was caused by the Finance team being moved into a new AD group and the old group being deactivated, resulting in GPOs mapping the Zurich printer as default [Data: Sources (2)]. 2) For a sample of affected users (one from Freelancers, one Sales rep, one Zurich Finance user), check: - their AD group memberships, - which location GPOs apply (printer provisioning / default-printer GPO), - whether their accounts are in any "unknown"/disabled state. - Rationale: this mirrors the diagnostic steps used in the documented printer incident where a remote user session confirmed incorrect default printer and comparison to a correctly-configured user was performed [Data: Sources (2)]. B. Remediate printing (documented remediation steps) 3) If you find incorrect group membership mapping printers to the wrong location: - Correct the AD group memberships to match the location/blueprint (remove the erroneous Zurich membership from Basel/Zurich users as appropriate). - Apply the Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard mapping (documented policy in the incident) and re-apply GPOs. - Force a policy refresh and test: in the documented case the team removed the incorrect group membership, ran a policy refresh, user re-login and the default printer persisted correctly [Documented action and proof: user tested, default printer remained] [Data: Sources (2)]. 4) If instead the symptom is that print jobs are being queued or disappearing, or printers are unreachable: - Check whether a Windows update/VM restore affected the print server shares. The documented fix in one case was to disable and re-enable the network share for the affected printer queues which released and printed pending documents [Data: Sources (28, 29)]. - Check network reachability between print servers and printers (ping/tests). If a new VLAN/segment was introduced, verify firewall/routing rules allow the print server to reach the printer VLAN. In the documented VLAN case, adjusting the firewall rule per the standard fixed the issue [Data: Sources (22)]. C. Remediate access (Freelance consultants locked out) - precedent and recommended action 5) Documented precedent: access issues tied to reorganisation were resolved by fixing AD group membership and ensuring the correct group assignments per the standard. Use the same approach to restore freelancers' workspace access: - Verify the freelancers' user accounts still exist and are active. - Check whether they were moved into a new AD group, or their prior group was deactivated (the documented printer case explicitly showed the old group was deactivated during reorg) [Data: Sources (2)]. - If they are missing the group membership that grants access to the shared workspaces, re-add the appropriate group membership or re-enable the original group according to your identity-provisioning blueprint. - After change, have a test user sign in to the shared workspace to confirm access is restored. Note: the general approach above is extrapolated from the documented printer/group incident (source 2). The records do not show a ticket of freelancers locked out of shared workspaces, so these steps are recommended actions based on the prior group‑membership fix [Data: Sources (2)]. D. Investigate the sales dialer (no documented prior case - recommended investigation) 6) Because the indexed records do not contain a prior dialer outage tied to AD/group changes, I cannot provide a documented fix from the records. Recommended next steps (explicitly recommended; not documented in the indexed tickets): - Check whether the dialer/CTI uses AD authentication, a service account, or ADFS/OAuth for authentication. If it uses ADFS or client app authentication, compare parameters to a working environment (see ADFS example below). - Verify the dialer's service accounts were not moved/disabled by the org-chart change. - Check dialer logs for authentication failures, and test placing calls while monitoring authentication and telephony logs. - If the dialer uses ADFS and you see authentication errors, consider whether ADFS client registration parameters differ from a working environment: a documented resolution in a different ticket was to align ADFS client parameters between environments (EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) which had restored connectivity for another application [Documented example: parameter adjustment fixed connectivity] [Data: Sources (11)]. These dialer steps are recommendations because no direct precedent exists in the indexed records. E. Communications and rollback options (documented precedents and recommendations) 7) Communicate the immediate mitigations to stakeholders: - Inform HR to pause further org‑chart/AD automation until root cause is confirmed. - For urgent printing of confidential invoices: temporarily redirect the Zurich finance print queue to a local secure printer or pause automatic printing until printer mapping is corrected. (There is no documented ticket showing a "temporary redirect" step; this is a recommended mitigation.) 8) If changes were made by an automated process and cannot be quickly remediated, consider rolling back the AD-group change to previous state (if you have a tested rollback) - recommended, not documented in the set. What to log / tickets to open - Open an AD/Identity ticket to list all AD-group changes since the HR update and to restore any mistakenly removed memberships (documented fix in the printer case was to correct group membership) [Data: Sources (2)]. - Open a Print/Infrastructure ticket to: - confirm the printer GPO mappings, - re-run the remediation used in prior incidents (group fix + gpupdate; or re-enable network shares if VM restore / Windows update affected shares) [Data: Sources (2), (28, 29)]. - Open a Telephony/CTI ticket for the dialer and attach call-drop logs; escalate to the team owning the dialer because there is no documented dialer resolution in the indexed records. Quick reference to the most relevant prior tickets (for handoff) - Printer default changed after reorg; corrected by fixing AD group membership and re-applying Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard - TI-1623017 (KF60) [Data: Sources (2)]. - Print server network share issue after Windows update + VM restore; disabled/re-enabled network share for queues and printed pending documents - TI-931293 (KF13) [Data: Sources (28), (29)]. - Printer unreachable due to missing firewall rule between print server and new printer VLAN; adjusted rule per standard - TI-1622483 (KF60) [Data: Sources (22)]. - ADFS client parameter misconfiguration causing connectivity failures; resolved by aligning parameters with TEST environment (EnableJWT & RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) - TI-1385257 (KF11) [Data: Sources (11)]. Bottom line - The most likely documented cause for the Zurich invoices printing in Basel is inconsistent AD group membership after the reorganisation (documented and fixed previously) - follow the documented group‑membership correction and GPO re-application workflow immediately [Data: Sources (2)]. - The freelancers' access issue is plausibly the same class of problem (AD group membership change); remediate by restoring correct group assignments (recommended, based on the printer incident precedent) [Data: Sources (2)]. - For the dialer there is no matching documented incident in the indexed records; open a Telephony/CTI ticket and investigate authentication/service‑account changes. If the dialer relies on ADFS-based authentication you may consider applying the same ADFS-parameter alignment approach used in TI-1385257, but that is a recommended diagnostic avenue rather than a documented fix for dialers [Data: Sources (11)]. If you want, I can draft the exact set of queries/requests to raise to AD/Network/Print/Telephony teams (who to ask for the AD change history, which GPO mappings to check, what logs to gather) - those would be recommended procedures derived from the above records, and I will clearly label them as recommendations.

question_text:
Hi IT, our compliance audit team flagged something on one of our project folders a couple of weeks ago and asked us to make some changes to it. We did the changes — at least we thought we did — and now things have been weird ever since, in confusing ways for different groups of people.

The most frustrating part is who is affected. Some of the external partners we work with on the project tell us they can sometimes open the folder and see the files, and then later in the same day they cannot. They say nothing has changed on their side — same browser, same network, same password as always. Then on our side, two of our regular team members say they can see the folder name in their file list but when they click any document inside, it just will not open or they get blocked. The files used to open fine for them just last month.

We have a specific user in finance who said her access keeps flickering: she had access in the morning, lost it around lunch, got it back briefly after restarting her laptop, then lost it again. Her manager already checked everything and her account looks completely normal.

And separately — though we cannot tell if it is connected — a totally different folder used by a different team has stopped appearing in their file list at all. They can still get to it if they have the URL bookmarked, but it is like the folder has gone invisible for some of them.

People are blaming us, the audit team, the project lead, and even the IT contractor who did some work last month. Everyone has a theory. What is actually going on, and how do we get this under control before someone blames the wrong people?
answer: Summary of what the indexed records explicitly show - Very similar incidents have been documented where a combination of (a) Unique SharePoint folder permissions, (b) a Sensitivity Label (AIP/Encryption) that referenced a different Azure AD group, and (c) authentication token caching produced intermittent access: users could sometimes access content (old token) and later be blocked (new token) [Data: Sources (30, 31)]. - Other documented causes for intermittent or partial access in the sample cases were: Conditional Access / device-compliance evaluation delays combined with cached tokens [Data: Sources (38)], duplicate groups with identical names created by a mix of Cloud and On‑Prem (Azure AD Connect) leading SharePoint to evaluate a different group object id than expected [Data: Sources (13)], and incorrect group nesting in Entra ID so effective permissions were not delivered to users [Data: Sources (20)]. What the records document you should check first (diagnostic steps drawn from prior tickets) 1) Confirm SharePoint folder permission model and which group the ACL uses - Check whether the folder has Unique Permissions and which group is listed in the SharePoint permissions UI (example: "SP_Compliance_Main") [Data: Sources (30)]. 2) Check Sensitivity Label / AIP encryption settings for that folder - Verify whether a Sensitivity Label with encryption is applied, and which Azure AD group is granted the AIP encryption key (example problem: label granted only to "Compliance_Core" while SharePoint ACL used "COMPLIANCE_TEAM") [Data: Sources (30, 31)]. - Verify whether the label was applied after the folder received Unique Permissions (records show labels applied after renaming/permission change caused the issue) [Data: Sources (30)]. 3) Inspect sign-in tokens / claims for affected users - Review Azure AD Sign‑In Logs for affected users to see which group claims are present in the token (records showed tokens containing COMPLIANCE_TEAM but not Compliance_Core for affected users) [Data: Sources (30)]. 4) Reproduce access behavior with minimal caching - Test access in Incognito/private browser and by signing out of all Office apps (records show Incognito/signed‑out sessions sometimes allowed access temporarily) [Data: Sources (30, 31)]. 5) Check for group origin / Azure AD Connect effects (if externals are affected) - In Entra/Azure AD, check the "source" attribute for users and groups (records found some groups/users "source = Windows Server AD" vs "Azure AD") and whether two groups with identical names but different object IDs exist (Cloud‑native vs synchronized On‑Prem) [Data: Sources (13)]. 6) Check Conditional Access and device compliance policies - Verify CA policies that apply to SharePoint or the project resources and device compliance/Hybrid AAD join status (one case showed CA requiring compliant devices caused delays; device status "unknown" and sync delays of 6-8 hours) [Data: Sources (38)]. What the records show worked as remediation (step‑by‑step actions actually performed in prior incidents) A. If the root cause is Sensitivity Label encryption conflicting with SharePoint ACLs 1. Remove the Sensitivity Label temporarily from the folder to stop encryption gating access [Data: Sources (31)]. 2. Reassign the folder permissions so the SharePoint ACL points to the correct group object (align group references used by SharePoint ACL with the group granted the AIP encryption key) [Data: Sources (31)]. 3. Reapply the Sensitivity Label with the correct group integration (make sure the encryption grant contains the same group/object id used by SharePoint) [Data: Sources (31)]. 4. Invalidate existing authentication tokens for affected users (have users sign out of Office apps/browsers or intentionally expire tokens) and retest - this resolved the token-caching inconsistency in the documented case [Data: Sources (31)]. 5. Monitor and confirm consistent access for all user types (internal, external, guests) [Data: Sources (31)]. B. If the root cause is duplicate groups from Azure AD Connect (external guests losing access intermittently) 1. Identify that two groups exist with the same display name but different origins/Object IDs (Cloud‑native group + On‑Prem synchronized group) [Data: Sources (13)]. 2. Consolidate group structure: remove the redundant Cloud group (or the redundant On‑Prem group) so SharePoint references the correct object id; clean the On‑Prem group as needed [Data: Sources (13)]. 3. Manage external guests as Cloud‑only members (so they are present in the final effective group) [Data: Sources (13)]. 4. Perform an Azure AD Connect full synchronization to ensure cloud and on‑prem state are aligned [Data: Sources (13)]. 5. Validate and monitor that external guest access is stable [Data: Sources (13)]. C. If the root cause is Conditional Access / device compliance token delays causing flickering access 1. Identify CA policy applied to the resource (e.g., "Require compliant device for Finance Resources") and note any recent adjustments [Data: Sources (38)]. 2. For immediate relief, adjust the CA scope (in the documented case they excluded the specific SharePoint folder from the Finance CA rule) or ensure device compliance sync completes quickly [Data: Sources (38)]. 3. Have affected users fully sign out and sign back in (clear token cache); trigger device sync for Hybrid AAD joined devices if synchronization is delayed [Data: Sources (38)]. 4. Monitor sign‑in logs to confirm Conditional Access evaluation succeeds and tokens contain updated claims [Data: Sources (38)]. D. If the root cause is incorrect group nesting 1. Review nesting in Entra ID and correct it so the high‑level group is nested into the permission group SharePoint evaluates (example: "IT‑Projekte" was still nested into old "SP_Project_IT_v1" instead of the new "SP_Project_IT") [Data: Sources (20)]. 2. Remove old/deactivated group, correct nesting, wait for synchronization and re-test access [Data: Sources (20)]. Which of the above likely apply to your described symptoms (match to your ticket) - Your description (some users sometimes see the folder then later cannot; locals can see folder name but cannot open documents; one finance user's access "flickering" after restart; a different folder invisible in file list but accessible by URL) matches the combination of: - Sensitivity Label + Unique Permissions + token caching producing mixed behavior (intermittent access, different results before/after token refresh) [Data: Sources (30, 31)]. - Conditional Access / device compliance delays (flickering that sometimes resolves after restart/sign‑out) [Data: Sources (38)]. - Duplicate groups / synchronization issues (external users sometimes having and sometimes losing access; folder invisible in list but reachable by URL can also point to group/object ID mismatches or nesting) [Data: Sources (13), (20)]. Limitations - what the indexed knowledge does NOT contain for your specific environment - The indexed records document the problems and exact remediation steps that were taken in those customers' environments, but they do not contain your tenant's specific group object IDs, the exact Sensitivity Label configuration in your tenant, nor your tenant's Conditional Access policy definitions and scopes. Therefore I cannot produce exact commands, object IDs, or the precise label/group mappings for your tenant - you must fetch those details from your environment before applying the exact changes [Recommendation: see next section]. Concrete, ordered checklist you can follow now (each step cites the prior case that shows the step was effective) 1) Verify SharePoint folder permissions and Sensitivity Label state - Confirm whether the folder has Unique Permissions and which SharePoint group/object id is referenced in the ACL [Data: Sources (30)]. - Confirm whether a Sensitivity Label with encryption is applied and which Azure AD group is granted the label's encryption key; check whether the label was applied after the folder's permissions were set [Data: Sources (30, 31)]. 2) Reproduce behavior with fresh tokens - Test affected user(s) in Incognito/private mode and after signing out of Office apps (records show this can temporarily reveal correct or blocked behavior) [Data: Sources (30, 31)]. 3) Check token claims and sign‑in logs - For an affected user, inspect Azure AD Sign‑In logs and token claims to see which group claims are present or missing (example: token included COMPLIANCE_TEAM but not Compliance_Core) [Data: Sources (30)]. 4) If Sensitivity Label encryption is found to reference a different group than the SharePoint ACL: - Remove the label temporarily, align the SharePoint ACL and the group granted the label encryption key, then reapply the label with correct group integration. Invalidate tokens and retest [Data: Sources (31)]. 5) If external guests are intermittently affected: - Inspect group origins and object IDs for groups with the same display name (Cloud vs On‑Prem). If duplicate groups exist, consolidate the group structure, ensure external guests are in the cloud‑managed group, and run a full Azure AD Connect sync [Data: Sources (13)]. 6) If access flickers and users are on Hybrid AAD Joined devices or CA policies apply: - Check relevant Conditional Access policy scope (e.g., device compliance for Finance). For immediate relief, adjust policy scope for the affected folder or ensure device compliance state and device sync are current; require users to fully sign out/sign in after changes [Data: Sources (38)]. 7) If group nesting appears inconsistent: - Validate nesting in Entra ID and correct any references to old/deactivated groups so the effective membership flows into the SharePoint permission group [Data: Sources (20)]. 8) After remediation, perform these validations - Test access for internal users, external guests, and users on different device types (company laptop, private device) [Data: Sources (31, 13, 38)]. - Monitor sign‑in logs and SharePoint access; confirm no "Item not found" / "Access denied" errors recur [Data: Sources (31, 38, 13)]. Actions that were actually performed in prior incidents and proved successful - Removing the Sensitivity Label, aligning the ACL and label group, reapplying the label, and invalidating tokens resolved the inconsistent access caused by label+ACL mismatch and token caching [Data: Sources (31)]. - Consolidating duplicate groups (remove Cloud duplicate or clean On‑Prem group), managing guests as Cloud‑only, and performing a full Azure AD Connect sync restored stable external access [Data: Sources (13)]. - Adjusting Conditional Access scope for the affected folder and forcing re‑authentication (plus device sync) solved flickering access caused by CA/device compliance delays [Data: Sources (38)]. - Correcting group nesting in Entra ID fixed permissions that had been incorrectly routed to a deactivated/old group [Data: Sources (20)]. Recommended next steps for you (these are recommendations based on the documented fixes; your environment specifics are required) - Inventory the affected folders, document for each: SharePoint ACL group name/object id, sensitivity label name and encryption group, whether Unique Permissions are enabled, and whether the folder was renamed or moved recently. (RECOMMENDED - not directly a record action, but follows the documented diagnostics.) - For any folder with a label+unique permissions conflict, follow remediation A above (remove label, align groups, reapply, invalidate tokens) [Based on documented actions (31)]. - If externals are affected, check for duplicated group names/object ids and run a full AD Connect sync after consolidation [Based on documented actions (13)]. - If multiple causes are present simultaneously (labels, CA, sync), prioritize safe steps: temporarily remove the label or temporarily scope CA to avoid blocking critical access while you align group membership - then reapply controls once alignment is confirmed [This sequencing follows how the documented tickets restored access before reapplying protections (31, 38)]. If you want, I can: - Produce a concise list of the exact data points to collect from your tenant (group object IDs, label encryption grants, token claim samples, CA policy names and scopes, Azure AD Connect health) so you can paste them back and I map them to the exact remediation steps shown above (I cannot fetch those tenant‑specific values from the indexed records) - I recommend this because the indexed incidents required those specific values to perform the fixes [Recommendation: you must collect environment details; records do not contain them]. If anything in the above is unclear, or if you can provide the following items from your environment, I will map the exact remediation actions (with references to the most relevant prior case) to your situation: - Folder name(s) and whether Unique Permissions are set (yes/no). - Sensitivity Label name applied to the folder, and the AAD group(s) that are granted the label's encryption key (if known). - For one or two affected users: a copy/screenshot of token claim groups or the Azure AD Sign‑In log entries around an attempted access. - Whether the affected external users show "source = Windows Server AD" or "source = Azure AD" in Entra. - Any Conditional Access policies that target SharePoint/this user group and whether those users are on Hybrid AAD Joined devices. References (examples of the documented cases I used above) - Folder renamed + Sensitivity Label conflict + token caching causing intermittent access; remediation: remove label, reassign, reapply with aligned groups, invalidate tokens (TI-1578891 / continuation) [Data: Sources (30, 31)]. - Token caching + Conditional Access (device compliance) causing flickering access; remediation: adjust CA scope, re-authenticate, trigger device sync (TI-1571046) [Data: Sources (38)]. - External guests losing access due to duplicate Cloud vs On‑Prem groups; remediation: consolidate groups and run full Azure AD Connect sync (TI-1583327) [Data: Sources (13)]. - Incorrect group nesting causing access denial; remediation: correct nesting and remove old group (TI-1569482) [Data: Sources (20)]. If you want me to produce the small, specific checklist of exact console locations and the minimal evidence to capture (token claim screenshots, group object id export, label encryption grant view) so you can collect them and post back, say so and I will prepare that checklist (this is a recommended next step - not part of the indexed records).

question_text: Hi IT, we've had a strange situation develop over the past two weeks where multiple unrelated-looking issues have surfaced across different parts of the organization. We cannot tell if these are connected to one underlying change or multiple separate things. First, when people try to open our internal HR portal or other internal-only websites in their browsers, they get a big warning page about not being able to trust the connection. They have to manually click through to bypass it. Internal websites only — public sites work fine. Second, our home-working population (about 80 people) say their secure work-from-home connection refuses to authenticate. The connection software says it cannot verify the server identity. They were working fine until a couple of weeks ago, and nothing changed on their laptops. Third, our office wireless network has been showing as unavailable for newer team members and people who recently got replacement laptops — the network just does not show up in their list of available networks at all. Their laptops are correctly registered in our standard management system. Fourth, when people try to set up their work email on iPhones — particularly people who switched phones recently — the setup goes around in a loop. The email app starts, then shows a message about device approval, then drops back to the start. The context we have: our infrastructure team did some kind of internal maintenance about three weeks ago, and our security team tightened some internal rules around the same time. We cannot tell if these are causing all four problems, two separate causes, or whether something else is going on. We need an analysis.
answer: Summary answer (what the indexed records document) - The symptom cluster in your ticket matches two separate, documented causes in the indexed records: - A PKI/issuing-CA change over a weekend caused multiple internal certificate-validation failures (internal HTTPS/TLS warnings, VPN/server certificate validation and LDAP-S issues). The prior incidents show that after PKI/issuing-CA work, services failed until intermediate/issuing CA material and certificate templates were corrected and certificates re-issued or re-enrolled [Data: Sources (55, 104, 13)]. - A tightened device/compliance policy / App Protection (MAM) change caused Outlook for iOS to require device compliance and therefore to loop during account setup for devices that Intune flagged non‑compliant. The prior incident was resolved by changing the App Protection policy assignment so affected users could complete setup [Data: Sources (20)]. What the records explicitly document (symptoms, root causes, and actions actually taken) - Internal browser TLS warnings on internal-only sites: - Symptom: internal HTTPS services showed "NET::ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID" / "issuer's certificate not found" (chain breaks at issuing CA) after an issuing CA renewal [Data: Sources (55)]. - Root cause and action: The issuing (intermediate) CA certificate was not present in the chain/AIA distribution points after the renewal; the intermediate CA certificate was published correctly to AIA/CRL distribution points and affected services were validated, which removed the browser warnings [Data: Sources (55)]. - VPN authentication failing for ~80 home-working users: - Symptom: IKEv2 mutual auth failures; concentrator logs: "client cert chain validation failed" / "EKU mismatch"; clients reported "could not validate server certificate" [Data: Sources (104)]. - Root cause and action: A refreshed certificate template used for the server certificate omitted the required IPsec/IKE EKU (included only Server Authentication). Resolution documented: VPN clients were reissued certificates from a corrected template that included the required EKU; new certificates were deployed via GPO and affected users validated [Data: Sources (104)]. - LDAPS/LDAP-S queries to Domain Controllers failing for some internal applications: - Symptom: Applications using LDAP‑S could not establish secure connection to domain controllers after PKI work; DC certs showed issued-by-old-CA and applications complained despite certs not expired [Data: Sources (13)]. - Root cause and action: Domain Controller certificate auto‑enrollment/template issues after PKI change. Resolution documented: Domain Controllers were re-enrolled from the internal CA after fixing the auto-enrollment template; LDAP‑S queries were restored [Data: Sources (13)]. - Outlook on iPhone looping during setup: - Symptom: Outlook iOS shows "device must be registered/managed" and loops; affected devices were marked non‑compliant in Intune after a tightened device-evaluation policy [Data: Sources (20)]. - Root cause and action: An App Protection (MAM) policy required device compliance before a protected account could be added. Resolution documented: The App Protection policy assignment for Outlook iOS was rescoped and affected users then completed setup successfully [Data: Sources (20)]. Assessment: are these incidents a single root cause or multiple? - The records show a common PKI-related cause for the certificate-validation failures across internal HTTPS, VPN, and LDAP‑S (all three are independently documented as consequences of PKI/CA/template changes) [Data: Sources (55, 104, 13)]. - The Outlook/iOS setup loop is documented as a separate cause driven by tightened device-compliance/App Protection policy, and was resolved by rescoping the App Protection assignment [Data: Sources (20)]. - Therefore, the indexed evidence indicates at least two separate causes (PKI work and stricter device/MAM policies), not a single change that explains all four symptoms [Data: Sources (55, 104, 13, 20)]. Step‑by‑step resolution plan based on documented prior fixes The steps below reproduce the actions that were actually performed in prior incidents. Each step cites the ticket(s) where that action was recorded as the fix. 1) Confirm PKI/issuing-CA state (documented investigative step) - Verify whether the issuing CA(s) were renewed and whether intermediate CA certificates are present in AIA/CRL distribution points. Prior incident found the issuing CA was renewed and the chain was incomplete in browsers [Data: Sources (55)]. - Evidence to look for (as described in the record): browser chain shows "Issuer's certificate not found" or certutil -urlfetch -verify reveals chain break. (This investigative action is described in the ticket narrative; the corrective action is documented in the closure) [Data: Sources (55)]. 2) Restore complete CA chain/publication (documented remediation for internal HTTPS) - Action performed in record: publish the intermediate/issuing CA certificate correctly to the AIA/CRL distribution points and validate affected services. After doing so, internal HTTPS services were validated and browser warnings resolved [Data: Sources (55)]. 3) Correct certificate templates and reissue/redeploy certificates for VPN (documented remediation) - Action performed in record: determine that the VPN server certificate had been issued from a refreshed template that lacked the required IPsec/IKE EKU. Remedial action performed: reissue certificates from a corrected template (including the required EKUs) and deploy the new certificates (the record states VPN clients were reissued certificates and new certificates were deployed via GPO) [Data: Sources (104)]. - After reissuing and deploying corrected certificates, affected VPN users were validated as able to connect [Data: Sources (104)]. 4) Re-enroll Domain Controller certificates if auto-enrollment/template issues exist (documented remediation) - Action performed in record: fix the domain controller certificate template / auto-enrollment configuration and re-enroll DC certificates from the internal CA. After re-enrollment, LDAPS queries were restored [Data: Sources (13)]. 5) For Outlook/iOS setup loop: adjust App Protection policy assignment (documented remediation) - Action performed in record: rescope the App Protection (MAM) policy assignment protecting Outlook for iOS so that affected users could complete device enrollment and add the work account. After rescoping, users completed setup successfully [Data: Sources (20)]. Where the indexed knowledge is insufficient - The indexed records do not contain any documented incident or resolution that directly explains "office wireless network not showing up for newer team members / replacement laptops" (your item #3). I cannot find a prior ticket or documented fix in the indexed records that addresses this exact symptom. Therefore I cannot provide a resolution from the indexed knowledge for the Wi‑Fi symptom. (No source ids cover this issue.) Recommended next steps (these are recommendations - not steps documented in the indexed records) - (Recommended) For the Wi‑Fi issue, check whether the office Wi‑Fi uses certificate-based (machine/user) authentication (WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise) and whether the new laptops have the necessary machine/user certificates or Wi‑Fi profile assigned by your management system (MDM/GPO). Also verify that the SSID is configured to broadcast and that any network access control (NAC) or RADIUS policy is not filtering by device posture, certificate template/source, or group membership. - (Recommended) Correlate device onboarding dates with Intune/MDM profile assignment and with any recent NAC or wireless controller changes. If your Wi‑Fi relies on a client certificate (SCEP/PKI), check whether the client certificate template or SCEP configuration was changed during the PKI maintenance (this is a plausible link to the PKI work, but is not documented in the indexed records). - (Recommended) If you suspect the Wi‑Fi symptom is PKI-related (e.g., supplicant requires machine certificate), try enrolling a replacement device and observe certificate provisioning and any RADIUS logs for certificate validation failures. If you want, I can: - Produce a concise checklist of the exact verification steps to run (which logs/fields to inspect, what to look for in certutil / RADIUS / VPN / browser chains), labeled clearly as recommended actions (not taken from the indexed records). Tell me whether you want those recommended troubleshooting commands and I will provide them. Key supporting record references - Internal HTTPS / issuing CA chain fixed by publishing intermediate to AIA/CRL; services validated [Data: Sources (55)]. - VPN failure due to template EKU mismatch; VPN clients reissued certificates from corrected template and deployed via GPO; users validated [Data: Sources (104)]. - Domain Controller LDAPS failures resolved by fixing auto-enrollment template and re-enrolling DC certificates [Data: Sources (13)]. - Outlook iOS setup loop due to App Protection policy requiring Intune compliance; resolution by rescoping App Protection assignment [Data: Sources (20)]. If you would like, I can map these documented steps into an ordered operational runbook for your team (separating actions that must be taken immediately from validation steps) - please confirm and I will prepare that, marking any steps that are my recommendations rather than documented past actions.

question_text:
Hi IT, our team did some kind of internal infrastructure maintenance over a weekend a couple of weeks ago — something behind the scenes that wasn't supposed to affect anyone visibly. Since then we have been getting a series of reports from different parts of the company that look unrelated but all started around the same time.

First, when people try to open our internal HR system in their browsers — to look at their records or report sick days — their browsers throw a big warning page saying they cannot trust the connection. They have to click through some 'advanced' option to actually get to the page. Same thing on a couple of other internal websites that everyone normally uses.

Second, our remote workers who work from home or while travelling — about 80 people — say they cannot get their secure work-from-home connection established anymore. Their connection software just refuses to authenticate. The error mentions something about not being able to verify the server identity. They were working fine until this maintenance work happened, and nothing changed on their laptops.

Third, the leadership team uses a special configuration for their email correspondence so that confidential communications can be cryptographically verified — outgoing messages they send carry a kind of seal, and they can read sealed messages they receive from external partners. That whole feature stopped working. They cannot apply the seal to outgoing email anymore, and they cannot read sealed email they receive. They were given new credentials around the time of the maintenance work, but those credentials do not seem to do anything.

Fourth, several of our internal business applications — the HR app, our SharePoint installation, and our network access control system — report that they can no longer reach our central user-account system through the protected channel they normally use. They CAN reach it through the unprotected channel, but our security policy does not allow operating that way.

These all started in the same window. Different teams are pointing at each other — the infrastructure team that did the maintenance, the application owners, the security team. We need an actual answer about what is happening.
answer: Summary (what the indexed records explicitly document) - The pattern you describe (browser TLS trust errors for internal HTTPS sites, VPN mutual-authentication failures, executive S/MIME signing/decryption failure, and LDAP-S binds failing for applications) matches a set of incidents that all began after an internal PKI/CA renewal or certificate-template change over a weekend. The prior incidents attribute these symptoms to CA/template changes and related side effects, not to application code changes [Data: Sources (55, 104, 36, 13)]. - Specific root causes and factual remediations recorded in those incidents: - Internal HTTPS services showed broken chains because the issuing (intermediate) CA certificate was not presented / published to distribution points; remediation was to publish the issuing CA into AIA/CRL distribution points and validate services [Data: Sources (55)]. - VPN server certificate was reissued from a refreshed template that dropped a required EKU; remediation was to reissue the server certificate from a template including the correct EKU and deploy new certs to VPN servers and clients (deployed via GPO/SCEP) [Data: Sources (104)]. - Executive S/MIME stopped working because new user certificates were issued without the corresponding private keys being available on the client: private keys had been generated on the CA side and key-archive/recovery plus local private-key import were required; remediation was new S/MIME cert issuance and private-key recovery/import on executive devices [Data: Sources (36)]. - LDAP-S (LDAPS) binds failed because Domain Controller certificates were auto-enrolled/renewed from the old issuing CA / template behavior; a fix to the auto-enrollment/template and re-enrollment of DC certificates restored LDAP-S connectivity [Data: Sources (13)]. What was actually done in the prior cases (concrete steps recorded) 1. Published intermediate (issuing) CA certificate to the AIA/CRL distribution points so client TLS chains could be validated, then validated affected HTTPS services [Data: Sources (55)]. 2. Reissued the VPN server certificate from a corrected template including the required IPsec/IKE EKU, deployed the corrected certificate to the VPN servers, and reissued/deployed or validated client certificates where needed; affected remote users regained VPN access [Data: Sources (104)]. 3. For S/MIME: performed key-archive recovery and re-imported the private keys to executive devices (and/or issued new S/MIME certs), then validated signed/decrypted mail operations [Data: Sources (36)]. 4. For LDAP-S: fixed the Group Policy / auto-enrollment template behavior and re-enrolled Domain Controller "Domain Controller Authentication" certificates from the issuing CA; applications could again bind over LDAPS (636) [Data: Sources (13)]. Step-by-step resolution plan for your ticket (mapped to the documented fixes) Below are the actions to take, phrased as steps that mirror the actions that resolved the prior incidents. Each step cites the record that documents that action having been performed successfully. 1) Confirm PKI/CA activity and scope - Verify whether the issuing CA (intermediate) and/or certificate templates were changed or renewed in the maintenance window. This is the common trigger in the records [Data: Sources (55, 104, 36, 13)]. - (Note: prior tickets show the issuing CA was renewed and templates refreshed during the weekend PKI work) [Data: Sources (55, 104, 36)]. 2) Fix broken TLS chains for internal HTTPS services (HR, SharePoint, others) - Publish the issuing (intermediate) CA certificate correctly to the AIA and CRL distribution points so clients can build a complete chain to a trusted root. Prior case: intermediate CA certificate published to AIA/CRL distribution points; affected services were validated afterwards [Data: Sources (55)]. - After publishing, validate from representative clients that the browser chain is complete and warnings are gone (the prior ticket documents "affected services validated" after publishing) [Data: Sources (55)]. 3) Resolve VPN authentication failures for remote users (~80) - Confirm whether the VPN server certificate was reissued from a refreshed template that omitted the required EKU. In a documented case the template omitted the "IP security IKE intermediate" EKU and caused IKEv2 authentication failures [Data: Sources (104)]. - Reissue the VPN server certificate from a template that includes the correct EKU(s) required by your VPN concentrator. Deploy the corrected certificate to the VPN server(s). In the prior incident, the new certificate with corrected EKU was deployed and clients were validated [Data: Sources (104)]. - If client/user certificates were required or need reissuance/deployment (SCEP/GPO), ensure they are reissued or available; the prior case redeployed client certs as needed via GPO and validated users [Data: Sources (104)]. 4) Restore S/MIME signing/decryption for executives - Confirm whether the executives' S/MIME certificates were auto-renewed and whether the private keys are present on the client. The recorded incident showed new certs were present but Outlook reported "you do not have the private key" because the private key material was not on the devices [Data: Sources (36)]. - Use key-archive / recovery to obtain the private keys or issue new S/MIME certificates with private keys that are properly provisioned to user devices. The recorded remediation was: new S/MIME certificates issued and key-archive recovery performed; private keys re-imported on executive devices; signed emails validated [Data: Sources (36)]. 5) Restore LDAP-S (LDAPS) for internal apps that bind to DCs - Check whether the Domain Controllers' "Domain Controller Authentication" certs were auto-enrolled/renewed from the CA/template change and whether the renewed certs are trusted by the application clients. The prior ticket shows auto-enrollment renewal behaviour triggered renewals and that auto-enrollment/template configuration needed correction [Data: Sources (13)]. - Correct the auto-enrollment/template settings via Group Policy (fix the template configuration), then re-enroll the DC certificates from the issuing CA so the DCs present valid domain controller authentication certificates. Prior remediation: DC certificates re-enrolled after auto-enrollment template fix and LDAP-S restored [Data: Sources (13)]. 6) Validate and close - After each fix, re-validate the affected functions: - Browsers: no more certificate trust warnings against internal sites [Data: Sources (55)]. - VPN: affected remote users can establish IKEv2/certificate-based VPN connections [Data: Sources (104)]. - S/MIME: executives can sign outgoing mail and decrypt received sealed mail; previously-signed messages can be opened [Data: Sources (36)]. - LDAP-S: applications can bind over LDAPS (port 636) to DCs without TLS handshake errors [Data: Sources (13)]. - The prior tickets explicitly record validation steps and note services/users were validated after remediation [Data: Sources (55, 104, 36, 13)]. What the indexed records do NOT contain (gaps you should be aware of) - The records document the types of corrective actions and their successful results, but they do not contain your environment-specific details (which exact CA was renewed in your environment, the precise certificate template names/settings you use, or the exact commands/scripts used to publish or re-enroll certificates). The tickets show the corrective actions but not the low-level procedure text that would be executed in your environment [Data: Sources (55, 104, 36, 13)]. - The records do not show any single-runbook that covers all four problems as a coordinated remediation plan for a multi-symptom outage in one customer; they are separate tickets that were resolved individually but with similar PKI-rooted causes [Data: Sources (55, 104, 36, 13)]. Recommended next step (not directly documented but necessary because the records lack your environment details) - Convene the PKI team (owner of the issuing CA) plus owners of Network/VPN, Identity/AD (for DC certs), and Application owners to: - Confirm which CA and templates were changed during the maintenance window. - Apply the documented fixes above (publish intermediate CA to AIA/CRL, correct VPN cert template EKUs and reissue/deploy, perform key-archive recovery / reissue S/MIME certs and import private keys, fix auto-enrollment and re-enroll DC certs). - Execute the validation checks listed in step 6. These coordination and environment-specific execution steps are recommended because the tickets show these exact fixes worked, but your environment specifics are required to perform them [Data: Sources (55, 104, 36, 13)]. If you want, I can: - Produce a concise checklist mapping each of the four symptoms to the exact remediation step and the corresponding ticket number that documents success (ready for handing to your PKI/Network/Identity teams). This would be based only on the indexed records cited above. Would you like that checklist?

question_text:
Hi IT, we are a couple of weeks into our migration from our old internal sign-in system to the new cloud-based one. The first batch of users went through smoothly, but since we did the second batch about two weeks ago, we have been getting a series of strange complaints from those users that we cannot make sense of.

First, several users from the second batch say they can sign in to their cloud email and Teams without any problem, but when they try to open our internal procurement system or our document management system (which run on our internal servers), they get an error saying their account is not recognized. The first-batch users do not have this issue.

Second, we are seeing strange inconsistencies with security groups. A user might be correctly in the new combined group on the internal directory side, but in the cloud they still show as a member of an old group. This is breaking some access permissions because the cloud and internal systems read different group memberships for the same person.

Third, the same affected users are saying their mobile email apps (built-in iPhone Mail and the Android Gmail app) keep asking for password and never connect. Outlook on the phone still works fine for them but the built-in mail apps just loop on the password prompt.

Fourth — and this only came up because someone noticed overnight reports were missing — several scheduled tasks that run on our internal servers using service accounts have started failing with authentication errors. The service accounts have not been touched by the migration as far as we know, but the failures started right around the time the second batch was migrated.

These all started in the same window but they look like four completely different problems. We need to figure out what is going on and how to fix it.
answer: Summary - what the indexed records show (explicitly documented) - The four symptoms you describe match a known set of incidents during KF70's second migration wave: (a) cloud sign-in works but on‑prem apps fail because on‑prem UPNs were overwritten to a new suffix; (b) cloud group membership remained the old groups because the new groups were placed outside the AAD Connect OU filter; (c) built‑in mobile mail clients failed because tenant Modern Authentication was enforced and those clients tried Basic Auth; (d) scheduled tasks using service accounts failed because those accounts were included in the tenant Conditional Access (MFA) target group and were synced/licensed unexpectedly. These root causes and their fixes are documented in tickets TI-1700001, TI-1700002, TI-1700003 and TI-1700004 [Data: Sources (22, 53, 4, 49)]. Step‑by‑step resolution based on the documented fixes Below are the concrete steps that were performed in the recorded incidents. If you want us to perform these for your environment we can follow the same sequence; where the records show actions taken I cite the ticket. 1) Fix "can sign into M365 but not on‑prem apps" (UPN suffix mismatch) - Verify whether affected users' on‑prem userPrincipalName was changed to the new cloud suffix (kf70group.local) while on‑prem apps still expect kf70.local - this mismatch was the documented root cause [Data: Sources (22)]. - If UPNs were overwritten, revert the on‑prem userPrincipalName values to the original kf70.local form for affected accounts (the record shows this was done for 25 users) [Data: Sources (22)]. - Trigger a full Azure AD Connect synchronization after the reversion to ensure on‑prem and cloud attributes align [Data: Sources (22)]. - Validate by testing 2-3 affected users against the on‑prem applications (the ticket validation step used 3 users before wider validation) [Data: Sources (22)]. 2) Fix cloud/on‑prem group membership divergence (AAD Connect OU filter) - Confirm whether the new group objects for the second wave were created in an OU outside the Azure AD Connect sync scope (the documented OU name was Migration_Phase2) [Data: Sources (53, 54)]. - Update the Azure AD Connect OU filter to include the OU containing the new group structure (the engineers added Migration_Phase2 to the sync scope in the record) [Data: Sources (53, 54)]. - Run an initial delta then a full sync to bring group objects and memberships into Entra ID and propagate member relationships [Data: Sources (53, 54)]. - Validate that affected users now show the intended group memberships in Microsoft 365 and that downstream access (SharePoint, apps) evaluates correctly [Data: Sources (53, 54)]. 3) Fix mobile built‑in mail clients failing (Modern Auth / EAS Basic Auth blocked) - Confirm that Modern Authentication is enforced tenant‑wide (this was enabled as part of the migration baseline) and that affected clients (iOS built‑in Mail, Android Gmail using EAS) are attempting Basic Auth [Data: Sources (4)]. - For affected users, remove the old cached credentials on the device and re‑add the account using a client/method that supports OAuth (Outlook mobile or updated built‑in client with modern auth). In the record they instructed customers to update the mobile clients to use modern auth and to re‑enroll affected devices in Intune where appropriate [Data: Sources (4)]. - Validate by confirming those devices can connect and that Outlook mobile continues to work for any users who use it [Data: Sources (4)]. 4) Fix scheduled tasks failing with authentication errors (service accounts & Conditional Access) - Check whether service accounts were moved into the tenant target group (KF70-AllUsers-Hybrid) that the baseline Conditional Access policy KF70-Require-MFA targets; sign‑in logs were used to confirm "Failed - Conditional Access requires MFA" in the documented case [Data: Sources (49, 50)]. - Create a dedicated exclusion group for service accounts (the record shows KF70-ServiceAccounts-NoMFA was created), add the service accounts to it, and add that group as an explicit exclusion to the Conditional Access policy so MFA is not applied to non‑interactive logons [Data: Sources (49, 50)]. - Re‑evaluate the Azure AD Connect filter to avoid unintentionally syncing service accounts (which led to license assignment and subtle auth path changes in the documented case) [Data: Sources (49, 50)]. - Validate by confirming scheduled tasks run successfully overnight (the customer confirmed success in the ticket) [Data: Sources (49, 50)]. Validation and documentation steps that were performed in the incidents - After reverting UPNs the engineers triggered a full sync and validated a sample of users (3 users) before wider validation, then confirmed all 25 users could sign in to both cloud and on‑prem apps [Data: Sources (22)]. - After updating OU filters they ran delta/full syncs and the customer validated group memberships and SharePoint permissions [Data: Sources (53, 54)]. - After changing CA exclusions and AAD Connect scope, scheduled tasks ran successfully and the migration runbook was updated to require explicit service‑account handling [Data: Sources (49, 50)]. - Mobile client fix required user device action (remove/re‑add account, re‑enrol) and confirmation that Outlook mobile continued to work [Data: Sources (4)]. What the indexed records do not contain / additional information we need from you (recommended) The prior cases show that similar fixes worked, but to apply them to your environment we need the same basic diagnostic inputs that were requested in those tickets. The records show the engineers asked for these items before acting - please provide them so we can proceed safely: - 2-3 sample affected user UPNs and the exact error messages returned by the on‑prem applications (Employee 30 requested sample UPNs in TI‑1700001) [Data: Sources (22)]. Recommended. - Names of the affected groups and the OU where the new group objects were created (TI‑1700002 used UserA/UserB and Migration_Phase2 as examples) [Data: Sources (53, 54)]. Recommended. - A list of the service accounts used by scheduled tasks (for example svc‑kf70‑rpt, svc‑kf70‑bkp01, svc‑kf70‑monitor were provided in TI‑1700004) so we can check their group membership, CA evaluation, and sync status [Data: Sources (49)]. Recommended. - Mobile device details (OS versions, whether the built‑in client or Outlook mobile is in use) so we can determine whether a client update or re‑enrolment is required (TI‑1700003 recorded that affected clients were built‑in iOS/Android and that Outlook mobile worked) [Data: Sources (4)]. Recommended. - If you can, provide Azure AD Connect configuration export (current OU filter), a sample of affected user on‑prem attributes (userPrincipalName), and the applicable Conditional Access policy details - these were used in the documented investigations and are required to make the same changes safely [Data: Sources (22, 53, 49)]. If you want, I can: - Apply the same sequence of fixes documented above (revert UPNs where overwritten -> full sync; update OU filter -> full sync; create service‑account exclusion and adjust CA; instruct users/devices to move to modern auth), or - Produce a runbook of the exact commands and UI steps to execute these changes in your environment (note: the indexed records describe what was done but do not contain verbatim command lists; any specific commands would be produced as a recommended next step and are not verbatim in the records). If you want me to proceed with the documented fixes, provide the diagnostic items listed above and I will follow the documented steps and report results back.
